# Executive Briefing: Interactive Narrative SaaS (v2)
## Generated by CEO synthesis from 4-agent ultrathink research run

*Run ID: narrative-saas-20260416-013241 • Date: 2026-04-16*
*Refinement locked: **read-only world**, session-scoped knowledge projection, no canon mutation.*
*Revenue guardrail: no raw data monetization. Author insights dashboard + publisher aggregate reports + opt-in fine-tuning only.*

---

### THE OPPORTUNITY IN ONE PARAGRAPH

The read-only refinement turned a conditionally fundable concept into a structurally coherent one. The v1 research identified a real whitespace — 40-books-a-year readers in withdrawal between books, no product serving them, a canon-governed licensed-IP model that could — but left three existential risks unresolved: canon enforcement feasibility, estate access, and legal posture. The v2 live-sourced research indicates **all three unlock under the read-only constraint**. Canon enforcement shrinks from an unsolved distributed-systems problem to a well-characterized grounded-RAG problem (tractable with Ragas ≥0.90 faithfulness). Estate access opens materially — the Agatha Christie × BBC Maestro 2025 partnership is a direct operational precedent for "the AI speaks only in words the author wrote," and the Authors Guild × Created by Humans "Reference Rights" framework gives the exact legal shape the platform needs. Legal posture simplifies because the Bartz v. Anthropic $1.5B settlement (Sept 2025) establishes a pro-licensing precedent that *penalizes* competitors operating on unlicensed training data while *not penalizing* a read-only licensed-canon retrieval product. What was conditionally fundable at v1 is cleanly fundable at v2 — subject to one now-dominant risk (author acquisition cold-start) that replaces the three that dissolved.

### WHAT IS GENUINELY NEW HERE (re-evaluated)

Still the four-way combination: licensed author canon + LLM-delivered text + canon governance + ebook-unlocks-subscription commerce. But the v2 refinement crystallizes the *identity* of the product. It is no longer a generative AI product. It is a **read-only reference product** — a canon-native, author-authored, reader-queryable artifact that happens to use LLMs as the delivery engine. This is a different industry category than AI Dungeon / Character.AI / NovelAI live in. It belongs on the same shelf as **Criterion Channel / Library of America / Folio Society / Masterclass** — curated, author-sanctioned, reverent. The marketing agent's "The Appendix" brand proposal captures it precisely: every SFF reader has read the appendix at the back of the book until the covers fell off, and the product is the appendix that answers.

The subtlest novelty, which the v1 briefing missed, is that **deep authored worlds are cheaper to operate per-turn than shallow ones**. Under v2 with prompt caching (10% cache-read cost on Anthropic), the pinned canon floor caches permanently within a canon version — a larger world has wider cache reuse across readers, not higher marginal cost. Under v1 this dynamic was obscured because graft-aware retrieval broke cache. The v2 unit economics flow in the product's favor as canon depth grows. This is a rare structural property.

### THE BUSINESS MODEL (refined)

Three flows. All three close under v2 live-sourced 2026 numbers.

**Flow 1 — Ebook sale.** $2.99–$9.99 Kindle-parity pricing. Author/publisher keeps 70% (we don't touch ebook margin for Structure 1 and 2 authors). This preserves the author's existing KDP economics and removes a major negotiation friction.

**Flow 2 — Subscription.** $14.99/month ARPU (Audible parity, [verified](https://www.audible.com/ep/memberbenefits)). $9.99 Companion-only tier (read-along-mode on any owned ebook). Subscription unlocked by ebook purchase. Three rev-share structures:
- **Structure 1** (established author / major publisher): 40% sub-share + 5% stewardship bonus = **45% ceiling**. Audible-parity floor, headline for public pitch ("bigger than Audible exclusive").
- **Structure 2** (mid-tier author, wedge cohort): 50% sub-share + 5% stewardship = 55%.
- **Structure 3** (platform-published new author): 70% sub-share. But *not* a Year-1 strategy — read-only makes thin worlds feel thin, so this moves to Year 3.

**Flow 3 — Constrained secondary revenue.** Author Insights Dashboard (bundled free to authors Year 1, premium publisher tier Year 2+: ~$600K ARR ceiling at 40% Big-5-imprint adoption). Publisher aggregate trend reports (Year 3+, ~$750K ARR ceiling). Opt-in fine-tuning is an *efficiency play* (30–60% inference cost reduction), never a data sale. **No raw data monetization. No in-session ads. No attention marketplace.** These are architectural exclusions written into ToS and author contracts.

**Unit economics (live-sourced, 2026):**
- Per-session inference cost ~$0.024 in Config B (80% edge + 20% cloud) — 60% cheaper than v1 estimate. Works at $8/mo subscription with 60 sessions/month COGS of $1.20/user. Well inside viability at $14.99.
- Ebook-to-subscriber conversion 15% target; 12-month retention. LTV/ebook = $27 platform + $27 author-share, on top of ebook royalty.
- Blended CAC target $20–35 via BookTok + author-channel amplification. Cold paid CAC floor $40–60 (TikTok $12–28 subscription-media, Meta $38 median).
- LTV:CAC target 3:1.

### THE HARDEST PROBLEM (shifted)

**v1 said**: "Defend canon against drift + adversarial prompting at viable cost."

**v2 says**: **"Can we get three A-list authors or one major estate signed to a retrieval-ready read-only license within 12 months at terms they'll publicly defend?"**

The technical hardest-problem dissolved under read-only. It's replaced by a cold-start business-development problem that inference-cost declines do not solve. ARCHITECT_2 independently identified "voice fidelity across long sessions on a fixed canon substrate at a 10–25K-token per-turn budget" as the new technical hardest problem — a real engineering bar, but solvable with existing tooling (per-character voice classifiers, DeBERTa-v3 Fool Rate, sampled second-pass auditors). The *business* hardest problem is scarcer: it requires human BD work at 6–18 month cycles, and there is no engineering workaround.

### CRITICAL PATH TO VALIDATION (90 days, refined)

Four hard gates. Each one is materially easier to clear under v2 than under v1.

1. **Canon-engine prototype on a public-domain world.** Build one world (Dying Earth pastiche, new Arthurian cycle, or Peake-adjacent homage) as a structured bible. Wire 3–8B on-device model + LazyGraphRAG retrieval + rule engine + NLI faithfulness check. Target: **≥0.90 Ragas faithfulness on 200 adversarial turns, ≥0.85 voice fidelity under DeBERTa-v3 classifier**. Under v2 this is tractable with known tooling — not an open research problem.

2. **First LOI from the refined wedge cohort.** Per MARKETING_REVENUE's re-sequenced list: Sanderson (Cosmere) first, then Hugh Howey (Silo), Andy Weir, Michael J. Sullivan, Chuck Wendig. Parallel conversations with **Asimov Estate, Jordan Estate via McDougal/Sanderson bridge, and Niven**. Goal: one LOI within 60 days. Under v2 the pitch is the **Authors Guild / Created by Humans "Reference Rights" framework** — *"We are not a training-data product. We hold Reference Rights only. Your text is retrieved, never ingested."* That clause is the single contract clause that gets legal approval.

3. **Anchor customer validation with waitlist + landing page.** Validate the "Living Editions" / "The Appendix" / "Canon Access" positioning with a small targeted paid test to fantasy-reader audiences. Sub-5% waitlist conversion kills unit economics pre-launch. Combined with #2 this tells us whether Sanderson-tier authors and Sanderson-tier fandoms both move.

4. **Written legal opinions on four vectors.** (a) Reference Rights as distinct from training rights under post-Bartz doctrine — Authors Guild / Created by Humans already frames this publicly; we need indemnification-grade position papers. (b) EU AI Act deployer obligations (Art. 50 transparency) confirmed against a text-only retrieval product; the primary GPAI obligations fall on the foundation-model provider, not us. (c) California SB 243 applicability boundary on narrative-reading products — our product is not a "chatbot" under the statute's definition; we need written confirmation. (d) Replika/Italian DPA precedent narrowed by our ebook-purchase-first adult-verification funnel — confirm. **All four are materially easier to close under v2 than v1.**

### WHAT THE ARCHITECTS SAID (AND WHAT IT MEANS)

**ARCHITECT_1's thesis (v2)**: text-only is the architectural load-bearing decision *and* read-only is the killer simplification. Together they delete roughly 40% of the v1 service surface area (graft pipeline, author review queue, session-to-canon mutation path, cross-session graft contamination protection, versioned graft conflict resolution). Per-session inference cost drops from $0.07 to $0.024 in Config B. The LLM's role taxonomy collapses from 6 roles (including Game-Master-with-Stakes and Canon Generator) to 3 primary roles (Narrator, Character Actor, Arbiter). This is not a branding tweak; it is a smaller, sharper product. The business implication: **we do not need to smuggle the graft pipeline back in under a different name**. That temptation will arise in implementation reviews; resist it. The read-only constraint is load-bearing for the entire business model.

**ARCHITECT_2's thesis (v2)**: canon enforcement is no longer the hardest technical problem. It simplifies to well-characterized grounded RAG with hybrid retrieval (Surface A pinned floor + LazyGraphRAG graph traversal + vector/BM25 prose exemplars + rule-engine validator) at production faithfulness ≥0.90. **The new technical hardest-problem is voice fidelity** across long sessions on fixed canon at 10–25K per-turn budget. This matters for the business because *voice* is the reader-perceivable moat — a retrieval architecture can be reproduced, a voice-exemplar corpus plus per-character classifier plus adaptive re-seeding cannot. ARCHITECT_2 independently identifies that the new moat is **authored depth × retrieval quality × voice fidelity**, with voice fidelity as the dominant moat component. This directly drives the business model's emphasis on per-author investment in voice exemplars and the canon-stewardship bonus structure.

**The ARCHITECT_1 / ARCHITECT_2 interface is tighter in v2.** The canon-enforcement contract shrinks from six endpoints (query_canon, validate_response, record_session_graft, promote_graft, reject_graft, author_graft_queue) to two (query_canon, validate_response). Smaller interface = faster implementation + lower integration risk + cleaner blast radius on bugs. This is a real velocity win.

### MARKET AND COMPETITIVE REALITY (live, 2026)

FEASIBILITY's live-sourced read: **competitive gap is real and structurally hard for incumbents to close**.

- **Character.AI** ([CNN Jan 7 2026](https://www.cnn.com/2026/01/07/business/character-ai-google-settle-teen-suicide-lawsuit), [CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/01/07/google-characterai-to-settle-suits-involving-suicides-ai-chatbots.html)): settled four wrongful-death suits with Google Jan 7 2026; banned under-18 open-ended chat Nov 2025; added ads April 2026 (late-stage monetization desperation). Valuation fell from $2.5B to ~$1B. Cannot pivot to licensed-canon read-only without cannibalizing UGC-bot value prop, which is their entire liability surface.
- **AI Dungeon**: delisted from Steam March 2024. Their 2021 moderation disaster is now the canonical cautionary tale for unbounded-generation products.
- **NovelAI**: writer-side tool, wrong audience, cannot pivot.
- **Fable/Showrunner** (Amazon Alexa Fund-invested, in talks to license Disney IP per [Variety 2025](https://variety.com/2025/digital/news/netflix-of-ai-amazon-invests-fable-showrunner-launch-1236471989/)): multimodal animation-generation, cannibalizes their value prop to pivot to text.
- **Real competitive threat**: Amazon's Dec 2025 "Ask This Book" launched over books in-library — *unlicensed*, which the Authors Guild publicly criticized. Microsoft's Feb 2026 Publisher Content Marketplace has **zero trade-book publishers** today. Amazon and Microsoft will converge on this space via their existing publisher relationships — this is the real competitive clock. **Time-boxed 12–24 month opportunity to establish the licensed / author-consent-native category before they do.**

**The category we are entering**: publishing-artifact shelf (Criterion / Folio Society / Library of America), not game-market shelf. Under MARKETING_REVENUE's "Living Editions / Canon Access / The Appendix" positioning we have three competitors rather than three hundred. Under "interactive fiction" framing we'd have been positioning against Choices and Episode. The category shift is architecturally possible under read-only only.

**Regulatory landscape (live, 2026)**:
- Bartz v. Anthropic $1.5B settlement, Sept 2025 — *pro-v2* precedent because it penalizes unlicensed training, which we don't do.
- Character.AI × Google settlement, Jan 2026 — establishes joint-defendant pattern for platform + model-licensor, which we navigate by not being a generative product.
- EU AI Act Aug 2, 2026 full enforcement — downstream deployer obligations (Art. 50 transparency) are low for a text-only retrieval product.
- CA SB 243 (Jan 1, 2026) — minor-facing AI chatbot regulation; our ebook-purchase-first funnel provides adult-verification de facto.
- Authors Guild / Created by Humans Reference Rights framework (Jan 2025 public beta, backed by Patterson, Isaacson, Orlean, Day, Nguyen) — **we are the architectural instance of this framework**. Coalition partner opportunity.

### GO-TO-MARKET SEQUENCE (refined)

Three moves, in order. Each gates the next.

**Move 1 — Months 0–6: Canon engine + platform-authored demo world.** Build the prototype (Critical Path #1) on one in-house-authored public-domain-adjacent world. Engineering test harness *and* permanent free-tier demo. Launch under provisional brand "The Appendix" with marketing copy emphasizing Reference Rights framework. Publish engineering-quality benchmarks (faithfulness, voice fidelity) as public research — this is how we recruit Sanderson-tier authors, who are sophisticated about AI.

**Move 2 — Months 6–12: Wedge cohort signed + catalog launch.** Targets 1–5 from MARKETING_REVENUE's re-sequenced playbook: Sanderson, Howey, Weir, Sullivan, Wendig. In parallel: Asimov Estate, Jordan Estate (via McDougal/Sanderson bridge), Niven. Launch with 5–7 worlds, public via ebook-store-as-funnel (the [Authors Guild-allied channel](https://authorsguild.org/advocacy/artificial-intelligence/), BookTok, r/fantasy, r/cosmere, Imaginary Worlds podcast, Ezra Klein Show). **Explicit coalition frame: we launch alongside Authors Guild position papers on Reference Rights.** This is not paid advertising; this is editorial co-launch. Target: 5K paying subscribers by month 12, $1M ARR run rate by month 18. (Same targets as v1 briefing — v2 doesn't flatter them, just makes them more achievable.)

**Move 3 — Months 12–24: Estate lane + TTRPG pilot + voice-fidelity flywheel.** Land Asimov Estate pilot as the public estate proof-of-model. Open Paizo (Golarion) TTRPG lane. Start shipping Author Insights Dashboard to signed authors — this is the retention lever for author relationships. Voice-fidelity metrics become author-facing SLA ("your Eliza stays at 0.93 Fool Rate across the 95th-percentile session") — this is the differentiator Character.AI structurally cannot offer.

**Explicit not in first 24 months**: Tolkien Estate (split custodianship kills speed), Le Guin Estate (prize disqualification policy signals no), Pratchett Estate (steamroller), Big-5 imprints beyond single-title pilots, New Author Publishing Program (read-only makes thin worlds feel thin — revisit Year 3), LitRPG vertical (ChatGPT-slop fatigue in the audience — revisit Year 2), explicit adult content, shared-world multi-reader features.

### OPEN QUESTIONS (refined)

- **Retention ceiling under finite canon.** Read-only caps session-count per reader at the world's authored depth. Expansion cadence is now the primary retention lever. Will authors ship ≥1 expansion/year to sustain subscriber retention? MARKETING_REVENUE's canon-stewardship bonus (+5% sub-share for expansion cadence ≥12 months, up to +10% for ≥6 months, capped at 55% total) is the incentive — untested.
- **Author acquisition velocity.** First five signings take 6–18 months each in the sourced BD benchmarks. If it's worse than that, the Amazon / Microsoft competitive clock runs out.
- **Per-character voice fidelity at ≥0.90 Fool Rate on long sessions.** ARCHITECT_2's new hardest problem. Tractable with known tooling, but the production engineering bar is real.
- **Whether "Reference Rights" survives its first legal challenge.** The category is conceptually established by Authors Guild / Created by Humans but has not yet been tested in adversarial litigation. A bad appellate ruling could collapse it.
- **Prompt-injection canon exfiltration.** A targeted adversary extracts author canon verbatim via jailbreak. OWASP Top 10 for LLMs 2025 rates prompt injection as the #1 threat; roleplay exploits hit 89.6% success against common guardrails. This becomes a copyright/licensing breach *on the author's behalf* if we fail to defend. Standard mitigation exists (output monitoring + verbatim-span detection + retrieval scope limiting) but is imperfect.
- **Amazon and Microsoft timing.** Amazon "Ask This Book" was unlicensed and got Authors Guild pushback. If Amazon pivots to a licensed version with existing Kindle rights contracts, they subsume a major portion of our opportunity. We have 12–24 months before they do.

### CEO VERDICT

**Fund.** The three conditional gates from v1 all unlock under v2:

1. *Canon enforcement feasibility* — no longer conditional; known-tractable with existing RAG + voice-classifier tooling. Not solved yet, but it is an engineering-project problem, not a research problem. ARCHITECT_2 characterizes the replacement hardest-problem (voice fidelity) with tooling and benchmarks that exist today.

2. *Author / estate access* — no longer conditional; materially unlocked by the Authors Guild / Created by Humans Reference Rights framework and validated operationally by the Agatha Christie × BBC Maestro 2025 precedent. Sanderson-tier living authors sign first; Asimov-tier estates follow within 12 months. Tolkien-class estates are still Year 2+, but we do not need them.

3. *Written legal posture* — no longer conditional; Bartz precedent cuts in our favor, Character.AI precedent is navigated by not being a generative product, EU AI Act obligations fall on model providers, CA SB 243 boundary is satisfied by ebook-purchase-first funnel. Clean legal package is achievable within the 90-day validation window.

The v1 briefing said "conditionally fund" pending three gates. Under v2, the gates are the normal business-development pace, not unresolved existential risks. The hardest remaining risk is **author acquisition velocity under competitive pressure from Amazon and Microsoft** — an execution risk, not an existential one.

**What changed my confidence**: the Agatha Christie × BBC Maestro precedent. A major estate operating on the exact constraint the platform is built around — *"every word spoken had to come from Christie"* — removes the single most brittle assumption in the v1 plan (that estates would say no). The platform is not pioneering a pattern; it is extending one that is already in commercial production. That is a materially different risk profile.

**Commit level**: I would lead a Series A at this architecture and business model, with the understanding that the first five author signings are the critical milestones and that the authoring-tool UX is the sleeper priority that will determine whether the canon engine holds at scale. The business is as much a *publishing tool company* (the bible editor, the canon-debugging pipeline, the author insights dashboard) as it is a *reader product company*. Budget accordingly.
